instructions:
1. Sit facing the pec-deck pad, chest against the support, and set the seat so the handle is at shoulder height.
2. Grip ONE handle with a neutral (thumb-up) grip and let that arm reach across toward the center, shoulder relaxed.
3. Keeping a soft, fixed elbow, drive the handle outward and back in a wide arc, leading with the back of the upper arm.
4. Stop when the upper arm is in line with your torso; pause and feel the rear delt squeeze, not the lower trap.
5. Return under control to the stretched start, keeping tension on the rear delt the whole way.
6. Breathing: exhale as you open the arm back, inhale as you let it return across the body.
7. Form check: keep the torso still and squared to the pad. Do not rotate the trunk to throw the weight back.
8. Form check: lead with the elbow/upper arm, not the hand, and keep the working shoulder down and away from the ear.
9. Working one side at a time lets you brace the free hand on the frame and chase a clean, symmetrical posterior-deltoid contraction; start light and only add load once the arc is smooth and the trap is quiet.

target_muscle: Forearms (Flexor Carpi Radialis, Flexor Carpi Ulnaris)
secondary_muscles: Grip Muscles (Flexor Digitorum Superficialis, Flexor Digitorum Profundus)
instructions:
1. Sit on a bench and rest your forearm along your thigh (or a bench) so the wrist and hand hang off the end.
2. Hold a dumbbell with an underhand (palm-up) grip and let the wrist drop, allowing the dumbbell to roll toward your fingertips.
3. Curl the wrist upward by flexing only the hand, lifting the dumbbell as high as the joint allows.
4. Squeeze the forearm flexors at the top, then lower slowly until the wrist is fully extended and the weight rolls back to the fingers.
5. Keep the forearm pinned to the thigh throughout so the elbow and shoulder contribute nothing.
6. Breathing: exhale as you curl the wrist up, inhale as you lower under control.
7. Form check: the only joint that moves is the wrist; if your elbow or forearm lifts, the load is too heavy.
8. Form check: get the full range by letting the weight open the fingers at the bottom, then re-grip and flex at the top.
9. The wrist curl isolates the wrist flexors on the palm side of the forearm; use a slow lower and a brief fingertip roll to train grip and forearm size together, and keep the reps in a higher range since these muscles respond to volume.

target_muscle: Forearms (Extensor Carpi Radialis, Extensor Carpi Ulnaris)
instructions:
1. Sit and rest your forearms on your thighs or a bench so the wrists hang just past the knees, palms facing DOWN.
2. Hold a light barbell with an overhand (pronated) grip, hands about shoulder-width, and let the wrists drop so the bar lowers toward the floor.
3. Extend the wrists upward, lifting the back of the hands toward you by contracting the muscles on top of the forearm.
4. Pause at the top where the knuckles point up, then lower slowly to a full stretch at the bottom.
5. Keep the forearms flat and motionless so only the wrists move through the rep.
6. Breathing: exhale as you raise the bar, inhale as you lower it under control.
7. Form check: use noticeably less weight than a palm-up wrist curl; the extensors are smaller and easy to strain.
8. Form check: do not let the elbows rise or the forearms peel off your legs to cheat the bar up.
9. The reverse (pronated) wrist curl trains the forearm extensors that an ordinary wrist curl misses, which balances the forearm and helps protect the elbow tendons; keep it strict and high-rep.

secondary_muscles: Forearms (Extensor Carpi Radialis, Extensor Carpi Ulnaris)
instructions:
1. Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with an underhand (palms-forward) grip.
2. Curl both dumbbells up toward the shoulders the way you would a standard biceps curl, keeping the elbows pinned to your sides.
3. At the top, rotate your wrists so the palms face DOWN (a pronated, reverse-curl grip).
4. Lower the dumbbells slowly in this palms-down position, fighting gravity with the forearm extensors and brachioradialis.
5. At the bottom, rotate the palms back to face forward and begin the next rep.
6. Breathing: exhale as you curl up, inhale on the slow palms-down descent.
7. Form check: keep the upper arms still — the elbows should not drift forward or swing.
8. Form check: the lowering phase is the point; take 3 seconds down so the reverse-grip eccentric loads the forearm.
9. The Zottman curl pairs a supinated curl up with a pronated lower, hitting the biceps and the forearm in one rep; use a controlled tempo and a weight you can lower strictly palms-down rather than the heaviest you can curl.

secondary_muscles: Forearms (Extensor Carpi Radialis, Extensor Carpi Ulnaris)
instructions:
1. Stand holding a barbell with an overhand (pronated) grip, hands about shoulder-width, arms hanging straight.
2. Keep your elbows tucked to your sides and your wrists straight and firm.
3. Curl the bar up by bending only at the elbows, leading with the back of the hands.
4. Stop near the point where the forearms reach the upper arms; squeeze the brachioradialis and top-of-forearm.
5. Lower the bar slowly to full extension, keeping the knuckles up the whole way.
6. Breathing: exhale as you curl up, inhale as you lower with control.
7. Form check: expect to use less weight than a normal curl — the pronated grip puts the biceps at a disadvantage on purpose.
8. Form check: keep the wrists neutral; do not let them flex or extend to help lift the bar.
9. The reverse (pronated) curl shifts work from the biceps to the brachioradialis and brachialis, building forearm thickness and a stronger overhand grip; keep the elbows still and the reps strict.

target_muscle: Forearms (Grip - Flexor Digitorum Superficialis, Flexor Digitorum Profundus)
secondary_muscles: Forearms (Flexor Carpi Radialis, Flexor Carpi Ulnaris)
instructions:
1. Stand and set one or two flat weight plates together on the floor with the smooth sides facing OUT.
2. Pinch the plate(s) between your thumb on one face and your fingers flat on the other — no hooking around an edge.
3. Brace your core, hinge slightly, and stand up so the plate hangs at arm's length by the pinch grip alone.
4. Hold the plate still with a tall posture, shoulders down and back, for the prescribed time.
5. Set the plate back down under control before the grip fails so it does not drop on your feet.
6. Breathing: keep breathing steadily throughout the hold rather than bracing in a breath-hold.
7. Form check: keep the wrist straight and the fingers fully extended flat against the plate — a curled finger turns it into a hook, not a pinch.
8. Form check: switch hands or rest as soon as the thumb starts to slip; quality of grip beats grinding out extra seconds.
9. The plate pinch is a timed pinch-grip hold that trains the thumb and finger flexors that hook-gripping never reaches; start with a single lighter plate and progress by time before adding plates.

1. Set a heavy dumbbell on each side of you and hinge down with a flat back to grip them.
2. Stand up tall by driving through your legs, finishing with shoulders back, chest up, and the weights hanging at your sides.
3. Brace your core as if bracing for a punch and walk forward with short, controlled steps.
4. Keep the dumbbells from swinging into your legs and keep your eyes forward, not down.
5. Walk the prescribed distance or time, then hinge down to set the weights back on the floor with control.
6. Breathing: take steady breaths against a tight brace; do not let the ribcage flare or the breath stall.
7. Form check: stay upright — do not lean back or let the shoulders round forward under the load.
8. Form check: walk, do not shuffle or run; each foot plants fully before the next step.
9. The farmer's carry is a loaded walk that builds crushing grip, trap, and full-body bracing strength at once; load it heavy enough that grip is the limit but light enough to keep a tall, square posture the whole distance.

1. Stand a heavy dumbbell on each side of your feet and hinge down with a flat back to grip them.
2. Stand up tall by driving through the legs, finishing with the weights hanging at arm's length, shoulders pulled back and down.
3. Brace your core and hold the dumbbells perfectly still — this is a static hold, not a walk.
4. Keep your posture stacked: ears over shoulders over hips, chest up, no leaning.
5. Hold for the prescribed time, then hinge down to set the dumbbells on the floor before the grip gives out.
6. Breathing: breathe steadily against a firm brace throughout the hold.
7. Form check: do not shrug aggressively or let the shoulders creep toward the ears; let the traps hold, not spasm.
8. Form check: stop the set when the grip starts to open rather than risking a drop.
9. The farmer's hold is the standing, static version of the carry — a pure grip and posture endurance builder; progress by adding time first, then load, and keep both shoulders level.

target_muscle: Grip Muscles (Flexor Digitorum Superficialis, Flexor Digitorum Profundus)
secondary_muscles: Forearms (Flexor Carpi Radialis, Flexor Carpi Ulnaris)
instructions:
1. Hold a light barbell behind your thighs (or in front) with an underhand grip, arms hanging straight and wrists neutral.
2. Let the bar roll down out of your palms toward the first joint of your fingers, opening the hand under control.
3. Curl the fingers closed, rolling the bar back up into the palm using only the fingers.
4. Squeeze the fully closed grip at the top, then slowly let the bar roll back down to the fingertips.
5. Keep the wrists and arms completely still so the movement happens at the fingers alone.
6. Breathing: exhale as you roll the bar up into the palm, inhale as you let it roll back to the fingers.
7. Form check: do not flex the wrist — this is finger flexion only, not a wrist curl.
8. Form check: keep the bar light and controlled so it never threatens to roll off the fingertips.
9. Finger curls roll the bar from fingertips to palm to train the deep finger flexors behind grip endurance; use a light bar, a full open-to-closed roll, and high reps rather than heavy load.

target_muscle: Forearms (Grip - Flexor Digitorum Superficialis, Flexor Digitorum Profundus)
secondary_muscles: Forearms (Flexor Carpi Radialis, Flexor Carpi Ulnaris)
instructions:
1. Hold a hand gripper so one handle sits across the base of your fingers and the other rests against your palm heel.
2. Set the gripper deep in the hand — not on the fingertips — so you can close it through its full range.
3. Squeeze the handles together by closing the fingers until the two handles touch or come as close as your strength allows.
4. Hold the fully closed position for a moment, feeling the forearm and hand contract hard.
5. Open the hand slowly under control, resisting the spring back to the fully open start.
6. Breathing: exhale as you crush the handles closed, inhale as you let them open.
7. Form check: keep the wrist straight and let the fingers do the work — do not curl the wrist to force a close.
8. Form check: a controlled slow open trains as much as the close; do not just let the gripper snap apart.
9. The gripper crush trains crushing grip strength through the finger flexors; pick a resistance you can close for clean reps, and on a heavier gripper a brief closed hold builds the strength to progress.

target_muscle: Forearms (Flexor Carpi Radialis, Flexor Carpi Ulnaris)
secondary_muscles: Grip Muscles (Flexor Digitorum Superficialis, Flexor Digitorum Profundus)
instructions:
1. Stand tall and hold a barbell behind your back at arm's length, palms facing away from you, hands shoulder-width.
2. Let the bar roll down toward your fingertips so the wrists extend and the hands open slightly.
3. Curl the wrists by flexing the hands, lifting the bar up behind your glutes as high as the joint allows.
4. Squeeze the forearm flexors at the top, then lower slowly back to the fingertip stretch.
5. Keep the elbows straight and the upper arms still so the work stays at the wrists.
6. Breathing: exhale as you curl the bar up, inhale as you lower it under control.
7. Form check: stand upright and do not lean forward — leaning turns it into a swing instead of a wrist curl.
8. Form check: keep the bar close to your body so the line of pull stays vertical through the wrists.
9. The behind-the-back wrist curl loads the wrist flexors at a slightly stretched angle behind the body, a strong forearm finisher; keep it light, strict, and high-rep with a full fingertip-to-flexed range.
